comments.body: Is the file embedded inside a JAR?
Note newer versions of Java have much stricter rules about what can be accessed if the module system is being used. See the Javadoc for getResource
@greg-449 correct. Not sure how to open the resources. Oddly, I can access MessagesBundle files.

Note that you're kinda doing it wrong. The right syntax is ClickImage.class.getResourceAsStream("/" + whatYouHave). Going via the ClassLoader will get you an NPE in boot contexts. Also, it's pointless extra code. The only difference is that the gRAS and getResource calls on Class itself are relative to the class unless you shove a / in front.

About your actual problem (not posting as an answer because I am unsure if it's right): [A] don't use the module system. Few do, it's annoying, and adds nothing. It's for the JDK itself. But, if you must, [B] The gRAS system is sort of designed around the idea that resources also follow the packaging namespace and concept. And module-info.java has an 'opens' that takes a package. So, open the 'package' that your resource is in. Here that is res.images. You may want to revisit where you stick those images.

You'd be wise to migrate your tree to a build tool compliant structure. 'res' is redundant really. You need /src/main/resources/images only, in which to place your image resources. Load with something like: contentPane.add(new JLabel(new ImageIcon(ClickImage.class.getResource("/images/ifx_micro_re‌​d.png"))));
are you sure it is not a filesystem issue? So do you have the permissions to read the folder res/images and the file ifx_micro_red.png as well?

Another possibility would be that you start the two pieces of code in a different folder, you should print out System.getProperty("user.dir") for both cases, just to make sure you start in the same folder, because you work with a relative path.

Files/folders and resources are completely different paradigms and shouldn't be and cannot be mixed. The only time you would treat an image file-wise is if the user were able to enter an arbitrary path for the app to work upon. I'm guessing that's not the case here. A resource path (url) should not map to a directory (such as "res/images") as that makes no sense

@g00se While that (use just 'images', not 'res/images') is a good idea, it has nothing to do with OP's problem. And it's not such a stellar plan that one should drop everything and refactor an existing resurrected project. I wouldn't mess with existing structures at all here, the gain is near zilch here. OP is clearly not trying to treat their resources as files, that's not the problem here. I think that's what g00se is getting at, but cyberbrain's advice about printing user.dir is completely irrelevant, that has no effect whatsoever on gRAS/getResource!

@g00se well when you do not package your compiled classes and resources into an archive like JAR or WAR or EAR, and do not have your project under management of a build tool, folder structure will have an impact. I agree with you that one should not rely on weird side effects.

I tend to discourage discussion of weird side effects of doing things wrong. Do it right. Do it NOW. If you like the mental exercise of wrestling with problems, you'll probably find you still have some problem to get your teeth into when you do it the right way.

@g00se It's fine saying "Do it right", but the trick is knowing what "right" is.  Using /src/main/resources/images doesn't work, because this isn't a Maven project. For a vanilla Eclipse project, the resources are in /src/res. The issue is not the location of the resources, but permission to access a particular resource.

@rzwitserloot you were right: the problem is with using modules. I set up a new 'non-module' project, imported all the files, and it worked. Just like that. Feel free to post that as an answer and I'll accept it.

@rzwitersloot I was using getResource rather than getResourceAsStream because I wanted to pass the url to iomageio.read (). It turns out that imageio.read() can also accept a stream, so I am now doing it that way.

If you have a module-info.java file then add an opens res.images; directive. If you don't have that file then all your packages are already "open". Or you could use Class::getResource--instead of ClassLoader::getResource--with a class inside your module and it will work in either case, even if the package isn't opened.

title: getResource returns null for a resource that does exist
body:
I have resurrected a program written in Java using Eclipse: I last worked on it in 2016. I am now using JaveSE-21, and this code no longer works- the returned URL is null.

String name = "res/images/ifx_micro_red.png";
URL url = ClickImage.class.getClassLoader().getResource(name);

res and res.images appear directly below src in the project tree.

If I add this code just beforehand, the file ifx_micro_red.png is listed.

Scanner scanner = new Scanner(ClickImage.class.getClassLoader().getResourceAsStream("res/images"));
while (scanner.hasNextLine()) {
   System.out.println(scanner.nextLine());
}
scanner.close ();

I don't understand how getResourceAsStream can find the file, but getResource cannot.

Update: I used the debugger to step into getResource, and found that it returns null because the module descriptor says that open is false. So, I am allowed to see that the file appears in the listing of the res/images directory, but I am not allowed to open it, and this (bizarrely) is implemented by returning a null URL for the file.

It seems that I need to put something like res open in the module_info file, but I have not yet found the correct syntax.
